title: The Macro Minute: Did “TACO Trump” just cede control of global energy markets to Iran, part III?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Delta to
present our macro minute for Tuesday,
March 31st, 2026. Hope everyone's having
a great week. So, as always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
latest morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
did Taco Trump just permanently cede
control of global energy markets to
Iran, part three?
Our answer is this terrible outcome
appears increasingly likely amid waning
support among US officials and US allies
for doing whatever it takes to reopen
the Strait of Hormuz.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis. Starting with
the growth cycle. The February JOLTS
data supported our cooling housing and
labor theme. The lowest private sector
hires rate since June 2009 strongly
supported our jobless recovery thesis.
Private sector labor demand is
contracting faster than total labor
supply on a six-month annualized and
year-over-year basis. In China, the
March official PMI data supported our
outlook for firming growth. In the
Eurozone, the March European Commission
survey data supported our outlook for
moderating growth. In Japan, the
February retail sales, February
industrial production, and February job
support generally did not support our
outlook for firming growth. In the UK,
the March Lloyds Business Confidence,
February retail sales, and final Q4 GDP
data generally supported our outlook for
moderating growth. Regarding the
inflation cycle, Wall Street looks to
close March with a relief rally amid
reporting that President Trump signaled
openness to ending the Iran conflict.
POTUS and his advisors concluded that
reopening the Strait of Strait of Hormuz
would exceed a four-to-six-week wartime
line, prompting a strategic pivot back
toward degrading Iran's naval and
missile capabilities and away from
reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff,
General Dan Cain, stated that US forces
are actively degrading Iran's defense
and industrial base, defense industrial
base, logistics, and supply chains to
constrain drone and missile production.
The three key goals initially outlined
by the US administration for initiating
the conflict. Additionally, Defense
Secretary Pete Hegseth Hegseth asserted
that diplomatic negotiations with Iran
are, quote, gaining strength and
emphasizing that sustained US military
pressure is compelling Iran to make a
deal.
Sustained disruptions in the Strait of
Hormuz imply persistently elevated oil
prices with a likely upward trajectory
over time.
Elsewhere, the January Case-Shiller and
FHFA Home Price Index data and February
Zillow Rent Index data supported our
cooling housing and labor theme. In the
Eurozone, the preliminary March CPI data
supported our outlook for firming
inflation, while the February ECB CPI
expectation survey data did not. In
Japan, the March Tokyo CPI data did not
support our outlook for accelerating
inflation. The Tokyo CPI data are
leading indicators for the nationwide
CPI in Japan and signal incremental
scope for expansionary fiscal policy by
the reflation-oriented Takayuki
government and a slower pace of monetary
policy normalization by the BOJ.
In the UK, the March Lloyds Business
Confidence data supported our outlook
for slowing inflation. Nothing
actionable with respect to our Bayesian
priors regarding the monetary policy
cycle.
With regards to the fiscal policy cycle,
the US and NATO allies are increasingly
resisting participation in the Iran
conflict, exacerbating fractures within
an already strained alliance. Spain
restricted airspace to US aircraft on
Monday. Italy denied landing rights for
US military planes in Sicily. And Poland
declined to relocate Patriot missile
systems despite US requests to reinforce
Middle East air defenses. President
Trump criticized non-partici- allies'
non-participation, warning that the US
would not defend their interests amid
fuel shortages tied to the Strait of
Hormuz disruptions, stating that, quote,
you'll have to start learning how to
fight for yourself. The USA won't be
there to help you anymore, just like you
weren't there for us. President Trump
singled out France as very as being very
unhelpful in a separate post following
US-Israel strikes degrading Iran's
military infrastructure and leadership,
adding that, quote, the USA will
remember.
And the reason we put this in the fiscal
policy session because, ultimately, what
less cohesion within NATO implies is
more of a desire It's going to be more
of a forcing function to get the NATO
allies to increase their defense
spending and their infrastructure
spending. Recall that last year
President Trump successfully negotiated
an increase in NATO's defense and
infrastructure
targets from 2% to 3.5% for defense and
an additional 1.5%
of GDP's worth for infrastructure. So,
by 2030, NATO is going to go from
roughly 2%
of GDP in expenditures on defense on
average across the NATO alliances to
3.5% of GDP on defense and an additional
1.5% of GDP on defense-related
infrastructure.
That's an enormous capital call for the
European economies, which, by the way,
are the US's largest net international
investment creditor from the perspective
of the Treasury bond market. And so, if
these economies are going to be forced
because the NATO alliance is breaking
down to invest more in their own defense
and more in their own infrastructure,
ultimately more in their securing
energy products for themselves out of
the Middle East, then ultimately that
just means the flow of capital that's
been supporting the Treasury market for
decades is going to start to reverse at
the margins. And And that's one of the
core features of our core research view,
which is is again the geopolitically
driven supply-demand imbalance in the
Treasury bond market, which again has
been our core research view since the
summer of 2023. So, this certainly does
not help
ameliorate that that that is
supply-demand disequilibrium. Getting
into the liquidity cycle,
accelerating private equity dividend
recapitalization signal investor
concerns regarding AI disruption are
increasingly weighing on the liquidity
cycle. Private equity firms backed firms
issued 94 billion in US leverage loans
and high-yield bonds in 2025 to finance
shareholder distributions, elevating
credit risk throughout the industry and
the economy per Moody's. Dividend
recapitalizations have accelerated as
private equity firms seek liquidity amid
constrained exit pathways driven by
macro uncertainty that is suppressing
M&A and IPO activity. Approximately 50
billion, or 53% of dividend
recapitalization proceeds, were
distributed to sponsors in 2025 versus
only 33 billion, or 34%, in 2024. The
remainder is allocated to debt
refinancing and general corporate uses.
So, very clearly, we continue to see the
lack of liquidity out there from the
suppressed M&A and IPO activity, you
know, forcing these, you know, private
equity firms and private credit firms to
have to go tap debt markets in order to
pay themselves and or pay their LPs. And
so, you know, this is something that is
tenuous and it's it's confirmation that,
you know, it you know, it's incremental
confirmation that the liquidity cycle
isn't exactly what we like it to be as
investors. And very clearly, Iran
removing petroleum products from the
global markets is causing issues from a
liquidity perspective as well. Recall
that the Gulf Coast economies are not
generating the same level of profit
revenue and profits they need to
continue supporting global asset markets
in so much that the net international
investment surplus economies over in
Asia are not able to generate the same
level of economic output they need to
generate household savings, corporate
profits, and foreign exchange reserves,
which are all recycled into global
capital markets. So, we're having, you
know, this this removal of the petroleum
products from the global market is
essentially a removal of liquidity from
global markets on a lag from the
perspective of the dollar recycling that
we tend to see
across the global financial markets.
Nothing actionable with respect to our
Bayesian priors on the positioning
cycle. Continue reviewing to learn
whether our institutional-grade risk
management overlays, K and Dr. Mo, are
evolving their signals in response to
these or anticipated fundamental
dynamics.
Transition to our 42 macro dashboard. As
always, we'll wrap up with the question
from our community. This one's titled,
are the markets missing the significance
of Hormuz? Says, "Hi, skipper. I'm
hearing the closure is a massive
disruptor for much of the world in terms
of oil, LNG, fertilizer, helium, and
that we're weeks away from critical
supply shortages. The 10-year spiking
and gold selling off might be signaling
a scramble for liquidity. Thankfully,
you've got us in a very safe stance." I
don't have us in a very safe stance, K
and Dr. Mo. I have us all in a very safe
stance, so let me be clear about that.
It doesn't look like the US has any good
off-ramps, and the Iranians are keen to
bring on the pain. Russia must be using
the situation as payback for the proxy
war in Ukraine.
Anything else we should think about
here, and what should we prepare for and
pray doesn't? Best regards." So,
excellent question. This is what we've
been talking about for weeks now, which
is, you know, the most of the markets
are focused on the this this being a
current account issue in terms of the
removal of these petroleum products in
terms of hurting the GDPs of of the
global economy, hurting, you know, the,
for example, Taiwan, who needs the
petroleum products from the Middle East
to help manufacture chips both from an
energy and also input standpoint. And we
know Taiwan is home to, I want to say,
98% of advanced semiconductor
manufacturing. So, you know, this whole
interconnected web
of petroleum products emanating from the
Middle East and being shipped across the
world
is a big deal. And to me, I've I've been
saying that the everyone's focused on
the current account. The capital account
is by far the much bigger story here in
terms of the removal of all that
revenue, profits, household savings, and
foreign exchange reserves, which are all
getting
all get recycled into global capital
markets. And so, in my opinion, just
this is just a
This is a function of time. This is an
x-axis problem, not a y-axis problem. As
long as the Strait of Hormuz remains
effectively closed, we're not
it's, you know, the the flows that go
through the strait are significantly
reduced relative to what's normal, which
is historically we're thinking about
crude oil is about 20% of global crude
oil products flowing through there. As
long as that remains effectively closed,
then we're going to have a liquidity
crisis. We are in a liquidity crisis.
That doesn't mean that you know, Taco
Trump can't you know, say what he needs
to say to get the markets up over I
don't know one to two or three day time
horizon, but the reality is as long as
Strait of Hormuz remains closed, there's
nothing that any
official can do to stop the liquidity
crisis from continuing to devolve. So
that's our that's our core belief and
our opinion. That's why we're in a
risk-off inflation market regime and
that risk-off inflation market regime is
going to persist as long as the Strait
of Hormuz remains effectively closed,
period.
Period.
If you have a dis- if you disagree with
that, then good luck to you and your
portfolio. So we'll wrap it up there.
Actually, we'll wrap it up with a
comment from one of our other
members in our global investor
community. This one says, "I wanted to
speak up for another subset of this
community, the chronically under
the chronically under invested verse.
So the reason I'm bringing this up
sorry, it's kind of a hard transition
there.
The reason I'm bringing this up is
because we are you know, we are in a
risk-off inflation market regime. We
have the We believe that the risk-off
inflation market regime will continue to
snowball to devolve as long as the
Strait of Hormuz remains closed
effectively closed. I have no idea how
long the Strait of Hormuz is going to
remain effectively closed. But I do know
that as long as we're correct on these
capital account dynamics, which we have
been correct on the capital account
dynamics and as we can remain correct on
those capital account dynamics, then
this risk-off inflation market regime
will devolve into a crash and and if if
not a crash and a global financial
crisis. And so that's been our core one
of our core beliefs as it relates
specifically to this to this conflict.
And so I bring it back to the comment
here because I think it's an important
comment in the context of that backdrop.
It says, "I want to speak up for another
subset of this community, the
chronically under invested and risk
averse. My family has saved and budgeted
our way to nearing seven figures in
liquid net worth with a paid-off home
without stock market exposure. I've sat
through the 2000 drawdown and vowed to
never do it again. I jumped out of all
stocks after the recovery. I stayed out
of the stock market even though I was
watching the exact low in the SPX. It
jumped 10% off the low and began and and
and being gun-shy, I thought maybe a
retracement a better chance.
I assume they mean would serve
I thought they would
they thought they would maybe retrace or
better chance to enter. That's my
assumption there. Unfortunately, I
stayed on the sideline second-guessing.
I remained there all these years and I
am I now I'm at 42 macro because it is
only one of two services that I know of
that worries more about the drawdowns
and the returns. I dipped my toe back
into stocks in my retirement account and
lost on this last de-risking signal in
my Roth where I have more products. My
GL- GLDM involvement overwhelmed the
stock market exposure, so I'm actually
positive. Overall, I'm down several
thousand dollars, but I'm out of the
recent uncertainty. Now I know I can and
will get back in. If it weren't for 42
macro, I would not be waiting to buy
back into stocks. I would just be happy
had I never engaged in reinforcing the
idea that it is better to be on the
sidelines. I don't think I am the only
one. So thanks. So really grateful for
this this positive commentary because it
reminds me and it reminds everyone else
that hey, Kiss and Dr. Mo are excellent
at chopping off the left side of the
left tail of the distribution so that we
can create a positively skewed
distribution in our portfolios in a way
that allows us to compound returns and
higher net asset values, which allows
compounding to work better because of
the exponential growth function. And so
Kiss does a really good job at that, but
what it also does,
Kiss and Dr. Mo, what they also do is
help investors to get back long after
the the the the crisis occurs. You know,
it's not just it's one thing that to to
to avoid left tail risk in your
portfolio, but if you don't have any
right tail risk, then you're never going
to achieve your strategic investment
objectives, which is to retire on time
and comfortably. Risk works in both
directions. You know, you're concerned
about downside risk when you're you
know, accumulating assets, but you
should also be concerned about upside
risk as you age because if you don't
accumulate enough assets, then
ultimately you're going to have a very
delayed and not so comfortable
retirement. 42 macro exists to help
retail investors use the same tools and
processes that institutional investors,
what we would consider be the world's
best institutional investors, help them
use those same tools and processes and
frameworks to achieve their strategic
investment objectives, which I have long
argued throughout my career that if
you're a retail investor, your only goal
should be to retire on time and
comfortably. Way too many of you guys
listening to me talk right now have so
many other goals. You want to trade
copper. You want to do this. You want to
do that. You want to do this. You want
to short that. You want to do this. You
want to do that. You all wanted to
succumb to your action biases and your
illusions of validity that you know,
it's okay to do all this stuff, but if
you optimize your investing habits for
achieving your strategic investment
objective and your strategic investment
objective is correct in that you want to
retire on time and comfortably, then
you're going to have phenomenal results,
particularly when you are using systems
like Kiss and Dr. Mo, which are very
similarly designed the way that many of
the top hedge funds that charge three
and 30 for their services across the
global buy side are organized. So we'll
wrap it up there. Darius Dale here
presenting our macro minute for Tuesday,
March 31st, 2026. Everyone have a
wonderful day out there. Best of luck.
We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
